channel.title: Genericity on OOFP 2025-26
channel.description: Recent content in Genericity on OOFP 2025-26

item.title: Type variable
item.description:
Type variable #

A (regular) variable stands for an underspecified value.

Similarly, a type variable stands for an underspecified type.

Example. Consider a function half that maps a list to its first half (say rounded down if the list has odd length).

The implementation of this function is identical for:

  • a list of strings,
  • a list of integers,
  • a list of Boolean values,
  • etc.

A type variable lets us declare such a function

item.title: Bound
item.description:
Bound #

In some scenarios, a regular type variable may be too generic.

Example. We may want to write an algorithm where the input can have any numeric type (integer, float, etc.).

A type variable in this case is too generic, because it can also stand for a non-numeric type (e.g. string, Boolean value, a function type, etc.).

In Haskell #

Example. The following function does not compile, because the operation + is not defined for arbitrary types.
